schools.summary: Reconcile Matthew/Mark's 'six days' with Luke's 'about eight' by inclusive reckoning; the three an inner circle present also at Jairus's house and Gethsemane.
Typological: six days = six ages before the resurrection; the three = 'few chosen'; the brightness foreshadows the glory of the risen righteous; Moses and Elias = Law and Prophets.
Calvin denies it was merely to fortify the three against the cross; with Gill, a confirmation of Christ's heavenly glory and earnest of the kingdom.
Chrysostom: delayed six days to spare the others jealousy and to kindle the three's desire.
Henry: the 'power and coming' Peter later recalls (2 Pet 1:16), a specimen of the kingdom.
Wesley names Mount Tabor as the site.

sides.position: to fortify the three against the scandal of the cross
a confirmation of his glory / earnest of the kingdom

outliers.note: Alexandrian-mystical: the Word appears to each according to capacity; only he above the world sees the transfigured Jesus.

schools.summary: 'Transfigure' = change of form not substance (metemorphothe: morphe vs schema, cf Rom 12:2); it occurred while praying (Luke 9:29); textual variant 'white as snow'.
The form of God shone through the form of a servant (Phil 2:7); Calvin: prayed not from need but to teach us.
Wesley: the indwelling Deity darted its rays through the veil of the flesh.
Cyril: this is He whom Moses saw, the Rock that was Christ (1 Cor 10:4); prophets saw him as each was able.

outliers.note: Alexandrian: asks whether Christ wore the servant's form to those below and the form of God to those on the mount; to be heard 'spiritually.'

schools.summary: Moses and Elijah = Law and Prophets witnessing to the Gospel; both had mystery in their deaths; they spoke of his decease (Luke 9:31).
Calvin judges them actually present, not a mere vision, since God holds bodies and souls in his hand.
Wesley: lawgiver, prophet, and the Father's voice all bear witness to Jesus.

debates.question: Were Moses and Elijah really present, or only an apparition?

sides.position: a vision shown to the disciples

schools.summary: A tabernacle is a tent; Peter, heavy with sleep and 'not knowing what he said,' wished to prolong the vision; not to be taken too seriously.
Calvin: Peter, captivated and fearing the vision would vanish, spoke foolishly without grasping its design (Ps 16:11).
Wesley: words of rapturous surprise, 'three not six'; Clarke: still expecting an earthly kingdom.

outliers.note: Asks whether Peter spoke 'as in a trance,' filled with a spirit — but not the Holy Spirit, since he knew not what he said.

schools.summary: The cloud is the Shechinah/divine presence (Exod 24; 1 Kgs 8:10); the voice echoes the baptism (Matt 3:17) but speaks about Jesus; 'hear him' rebukes Peter.
'Hear ye him' = the shadow of Law and Prophets passing for the one light of the Gospel, the Prophet Moses foretold (Deut 18:18); the Trinity manifested.
Chrysostom: a bright (not terrifying) cloud to teach; the Father speaks so they know the voice is from God.
Calvin: their eyes covered in mercy, teaching humility — they were not yet ready for the heavenly brightness.
Wesley: hear him as superior to Moses and the prophets (Deut 18:15).

outliers.note: The bright cloud may be the Father's power, or the Holy Spirit, or the Saviour himself overshadowing Law, Prophets, and Gospel.

schools.summary: Awe at the divine presence; none can see God and live (2 Cor 4:6); set beside Israel at Moses' face and the priests in the Temple, and Daniel and Saul who fell.
Calvin: God intended the terror to fix the vision in memory; his perceived majesty must cast us down.
Origen: the three could not bear the glory of the voice and fell, beseeching God.
The touch is brotherly tenderness — the familiar hand and words (Matt 14:27,31); paralleled by Daniel touched and set upright; from Jesus alone come and are borne divine communications.
Christ acts as Mediator, raising the fallen so believers may boldly enter God's presence and not be terrified by his majesty.
Wesley: the same moment he gave them courage and strength.
Moses and Elijah gone in the cloud; 'Jesus only,' after 'Be not afraid,' rebukes Peter's wish — ecstasy is rare and it is good to return to the familiar Christ.
Calvin: the Law and Prophets had a temporary glory that Christ alone might remain in view; be led by Moses's hand to Christ; condemns confounding Christ with the saints.
Silence till the resurrection: the vision is confirming evidence for after the cross; telling it now would provoke the Jews; the ban included the other apostles.
Calvin: they would not have believed without the resurrection; afterward the vision proved he kept his divinity entire under the flesh (Phil 2:7).
Wesley: keep silent lest the others be grieved and outsiders enraged or unbelieving — till the resurrection make it credible.
Cyril: the coming three-day resurrection is prefigured by Jonah (Matt 12:40).
The scribal tradition rests on Malachi 4:5-6; the question came from the three, puzzled by Elijah's appearing and vanishing; abundant Jewish parallels.
Chrysostom: the scribes, blinding the people, taught only the second coming and so misled them about Elijah (cf John 1:21; 4:25).
Jerome: they took the vision for his glorious coming; Augustine reads 'restore all things' of Antichrist's victims; John foreshadowed as Elias.
Calvin: they wrongly imagined the resurrection to be the commencement of the kingdom.

outliers.note: Forbids transmigration: John came 'in the spirit and power of Elias,' not in Elijah's soul.

schools.summary: 'Restore' = heal, correct, set in order (cf Matt 12:13; Mal 4:6 LXX); restores not to the former but a better, Gospel state (Acts 3:21).
Calvin: Malachi's word is true but misread; John, restoring the Church, was named Elijah, not the Tishbite returned in person.
Wesley: Elijah was to regulate all things in order to the coming of Christ.

debates.question: Does 'Elias shall come' await a literal future Elijah, or was it fulfilled in John?

sides.position: the words remain open to a literal future Elijah

schools.summary: John is the promised Elijah in spirit and power (Luke 1:17), not in person (John 1:21); 'knew him not' = failed to recognise him, as they fail to know Jesus; he was killed (Matt 14:10).
Wesley: John's 'I am not Elijah' denied being the Tishbite returned, not being the one Malachi foretold under that name.

outliers.note: Bars transmigration: the soul of Elijah and of John were not one and the same (the angel's word to Zacharias).

themes: rejection prefigures the Son of man

schools.summary: The disciples grasp that John was Elijah in spirit, drawing it from Christ's words on the sufferings of Elijah and of himself; a frank owning of how slowly they passed from scribal literalism to the spiritual sense.
Gill: by his saying Elias was come and recounting his ill usage, it was clear to them.
It was the next day (Luke 9:37); below, scribes were questioning the disciples (Mark 9:14); Raphael's painting wrongly synchronises the two scenes.

Allegory of the descent: Jesus, since charity seeks not her own, comes down from the mount of his divinity to the weak; the father covertly accuses the Apostles; fire and water = anger and fleshly lust.
Chrysostom: the man appeals against the disciples, but Christ clears them, charging the failure to the patient's weak faith.
Henry: Christ's glories do not make him unmindful of our miseries; he came down easy of access.
'Lord' = 'Sir'; the boy an only child, 'lunatic' (seleniazetai, 'moonstruck'); the symptoms those of epilepsy with insanity; the demon hid under a natural disorder.
Wesley: 'lunatic' used with propriety though the case was preternatural — the spirit exploited the moon's influence on brain and nerves.
Gill: the father, addressing Christ with honour, craves mercy for his only child in a miserable case.

The failure lay in want of faith, not in Christ's commission (Matt 17:20); the disciples trusted the formula of the Name without the spiritual power; brought to the nine while Christ was on the mount.
Gill: the father meant to bring the boy to Christ, but, finding him absent, applied to the nine, who attempted the cure without success.
'Perverse' (diestrammene) = distorted, twisted from the truth; the words touch scribes and disciples both, who lacked the faith that prays and wrangled over the boy's misery.
Calvin and Gill: the rebuke is aimed at the scribes, who through inveterate malice resist God, not at the disciples.
Wesley: spoken principally to the disciples; Clarke: to disciples, father, and the whole unbelieving multitude.

debates.question: To whom is 'O faithless and perverse generation' addressed?

outliers.note: Hears the rebuke as the Saviour's grief over the whole race of men, oppressed by their wickedness.

schools.summary: 'Rebuke' joins reproof and command (Mark's words, Mark 9:25); the spirit cried and convulsed the boy, who seemed dead till Jesus raised him; the sufferer was past childhood.
Gill: the rebuke fell on the devil, not the father (already reproved) nor the child.

When prayer fails the fault is the asker's (Matt 21:22); the mustard-seed faith is great and entire, not little (1 Cor 13:2); fasting and prayer read broadly; 'this mountain' = the proud devil or earthly pride.
Chrysostom: the disciples feared they had forfeited the grace once given; their faith was weak, yet not all.
Origen: faith is like a mustard-seed — despised and mean, yet in a good heart it becomes a great tree.
The question was put privately in the house; the failure traced to unbelief; Clarke turns it on the minister to search himself by private prayer.
Calvin: they lost the power by their own fault; God never forsakes us if we keep the door open to his grace.

debates.question: Is faith 'as a grain of mustard seed' little faith or great faith?

sides.position: great, entire faith (the seed despised but mighty)
the smallest measure of genuine faith

schools.summary: Many MSS read 'little faith' for 'unbelief'; the mustard-seed = either the feeblest genuine faith or a growing faith (Matt 13:31); the saying hyperbolical (Matt 21:21); 'this mountain' a parable of our besetting obstacle.
Cyril: faith small in size but fiery in operation, working the greatest effects in the soul in a moment.
Wesley: this miracle-faith is not always saving (1 Cor 13:2) — a supernatural persuasion that God will work by one at that hour.
Gill: 'unbelief' = the smallness of the disciples' faith, charged to them, not the father.

debates.question: What does faith 'as a grain of mustard seed' mean?

sides.position: the feeblest measure of genuine faith
a small but growing, expanding faith

schools.summary: 'This kind' = a generic class of fiercer possession, expelled only with prayer and fasting that keep faith vigorous; Clarke notes the whole verse is absent from the Vatican MS.
Calvin: 'this kind of devils' reproves negligence — serious conflict with Satan demands vigorous effort; Gill: a worst, obstinate kind.
Wesley: a strong testimony to the efficacy of fasting joined to fervent prayer.

debates.question: Does 'this kind' refer to the demons or to faith?

sides.position: this kind of faith (which goes not out but by prayer and fasting)

schools.summary: Often foretold so the event would fall lighter; Christ mixes the grievous and the joyful (rising the third day); their sorrow sprang from love, not unbelief.
On the verb: Barnes takes 'betray' of Judas (Matt 26:14); Clarke prefers 'delivered up,' the agency God's; Ellicott: 'as they went to and fro,' he would not that any know (Mark 9:30).
Calvin: the nearer the death, the more often he warned them, lest the spectacle shock their faith; Henry: foreknowing all, he undertook redemption — commending his love.
Cyril: he came to his passion willingly, foreknowing it, lest the world perish in its sins.

debates.question: Whose is the agency in 'delivered/betrayed into the hands of men'?

sides.position: God the Father delivering him up (Rom 8:32)

schools.summary: The third-day rising (Matt 12:40) joined to grief; they understood not, were afraid to ask (Mark 9:32), and learned slowly and reluctantly; the sorrow shows they grasped his death and resurrection.
Gill: Christ told them plainly, while still in Galilee, that he should be crucified.
The 'tribute' is the didrachma, the half-shekel Temple-rate (Exod 30:13; 2 Chr 24:9), not the Roman tax — a voluntary church-rate none could be compelled to pay.
Chrysostom traces it to the firstborn's redemption-money (Num 3:44); Hilary figures Stephen in the coin; Augustine: the children of the kingdom are free; Gregory: avoid scandal where truth allows.
Calvin: by paying of his own accord Christ declared his subjection (Phil 2:7), yet showed it free and voluntary, not of necessity; they came to Peter as Christ lived in his house.
Henry: not a civil Roman tax but church-dues; Christ came to Capernaum ready to pay them.

outliers.note: Kings' children are free, strangers not; the sons of God, strangers to earthly kings, are the free ones.

schools.summary: 'Prevented' = anticipated, went before (proephthasen; archaic AV usage), showing Christ knew what Peter had said; Peter answered readily, as Christ had paid before, without weighing the altered case.
Calvin: Peter's 'Yes' a modest excuse — Christ had been accustomed to pay; they addressed Peter because Christ lived with him.
Clarke: Christ customarily paid taxes; God's children are subject to civil law and should pay, their task being the morals of the world, not its politics.
Cyril: under the sonship lies the deeper truth — the Father of our Lord, 'Thou art My Son' (Ps 2:7).

outliers.note: Allegorises: the kings' sons become the sons of God, free because they have known the truth.

schools.summary: 'The sons' = Christ (and the disciples, against the Jews), logically free from the temple tax but not practically; Eastern taxation was hateful as a sign of subjugation (1 Kgs 12:4).
Clarke and Wesley spell the syllogism: the tax is for the house of God; Christ is the Son of God; therefore he is free, and his disciples exempt like ministering priests.
Gill records Peter's answer.

Not obliged but best to pay, lest they seem to despise the temple; the stater = one shekel, just enough for two; 'lest we cause them to stumble'; the only NT hook-fishing; a tone of gentle irony (cf Matt 15:12).
Calvin: not poverty but a miracle to prove Christ's dominion over creation, greater than earthly kings, with the fish his tributaries; Gill: he forwent his right to give no offence.
Wesley: yield even an unjust demand for peace and love, in anything not forbidden; note the tender 'for me and thee.'
